quiz_questions.question_text: Arteries carry blood:

options.text: Toward the heart
Away from the heart
Only to the lungs
Only within the liver

quiz_questions.explanation: By definition, arteries carry blood away from the heart regardless of whether the blood is oxygenated or deoxygenated, such as the pulmonary arteries carrying deoxygenated blood to the lungs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Veins carry blood:

options.text: Away from the heart
Only deoxygenated blood
Toward the heart
Only from the lungs

quiz_questions.explanation: Veins are defined as vessels that carry blood toward the heart, regardless of oxygen content; for example, pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left atrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: Capillaries are the site of:

options.text: Blood storage
Hormone production
Gas, nutrient, and waste exchange between blood and tissues
Blood cell production

quiz_questions.explanation: Capillaries have walls that are only one endothelial cell thick, which allows efficient diffusion of oxygen, carbon dioxide, nutrients, and metabolic wastes between the blood and surrounding tissues.

quiz_questions.question_text: Arteries have thicker walls than veins primarily because they must:

options.text: Carry more blood volume
Withstand higher blood pressure
Contain valves to prevent backflow
Produce red blood cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Arteries experience much higher pressures than veins because they receive blood directly from the pumping ventricles, so their walls contain more smooth muscle and elastic tissue in the tunica media.

quiz_questions.question_text: Veins contain valves to:

options.text: Increase blood pressure
Speed up blood flow
Prevent backflow of blood, especially against gravity
Filter waste products from the blood

quiz_questions.explanation: Venous blood flows under low pressure, so one-way valves in the veins prevent blood from pooling or flowing backward, which is particularly important in the lower extremities where blood must return upward against gravity.

quiz_questions.question_text: The largest artery in the body is the:

options.text: Pulmonary artery
Femoral artery
Aorta
Carotid artery

quiz_questions.explanation: The aorta is the largest artery in the body, originating from the left ventricle and distributing oxygenated blood to all systemic arteries throughout the body.

quiz_questions.question_text: Arterioles regulate blood flow to capillary beds through:

options.text: Valves within the vessel
Smooth muscle constriction and dilation
Changes in blood cell production
Hormones released from the vessel wall

quiz_questions.explanation: Arterioles are known as resistance vessels because their smooth muscle walls can constrict or dilate to precisely control the volume of blood entering each capillary bed, thereby regulating local tissue perfusion.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the pulmonary circuit, the pulmonary arteries carry:

options.text: Oxygenated blood to the body
Deoxygenated blood to the lungs
Blood to the kidneys
Oxygenated blood to the brain

quiz_questions.explanation: The pulmonary arteries are a notable exception to the general rule that arteries carry oxygenated blood; they transport deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs for gas exchange.

quiz_questions.question_text: The hepatic portal system carries blood from the:

options.text: Heart directly to the liver
GI tract to the liver for processing before entering general circulation
Liver directly to the heart
Kidneys to the liver

quiz_questions.explanation: The hepatic portal vein carries nutrient-rich blood from the gastrointestinal tract to the liver, where nutrients are processed, toxins are detoxified, and substances are metabolized before blood enters the systemic circulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Blood returns to the heart from the legs primarily with help from:

options.text: Gravity alone
The skeletal muscle pump and venous valves
Arterial blood pressure pushing through capillaries
The liver pumping blood upward

quiz_questions.explanation: The skeletal muscle pump works by contracting leg muscles that squeeze veins, pushing blood upward, while one-way venous valves prevent backflow between contractions, together overcoming the force of gravity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is vasoconstriction?

options.text: The widening of blood vessels that decreases blood pressure
The narrowing of blood vessels due to smooth muscle contraction, increasing blood pressure
The formation of new blood vessels
The hardening of artery walls from plaque

quiz_questions.explanation: Vasoconstriction is the contraction of smooth muscle in the vessel wall that narrows the lumen, increasing peripheral resistance and raising blood pressure while reducing local blood flow.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is vasodilation?

options.text: The narrowing of blood vessels that raises blood pressure
The widening of blood vessels due to smooth muscle relaxation, decreasing blood pressure
The destruction of blood vessel walls
The clotting of blood within a vessel

quiz_questions.explanation: Vasodilation occurs when smooth muscle in the vessel wall relaxes, enlarging the lumen, which decreases peripheral resistance and blood pressure while increasing local blood flow to tissues.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is atherosclerosis?

options.text: A normal age-related change in blood vessels
A disease characterized by plaque buildup of fats and cholesterol in artery walls, narrowing the lumen
An infection of the veins
A condition where valves in veins fail

quiz_questions.explanation: Atherosclerosis is a chronic inflammatory disease in which fatty plaques accumulate within arterial walls, progressively narrowing the lumen and restricting blood flow, and is a leading cause of heart attacks and strokes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an aneurysm?

options.text: A blood clot within a deep vein
An abnormal bulging or ballooning of a weakened arterial wall
A failure of venous valves
A normal expansion of vessels during exercise

quiz_questions.explanation: An aneurysm is a localized, pathological dilation of an arterial wall caused by weakness in the vessel, and it carries a serious risk of rupture leading to life-threatening internal hemorrhage.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is deep vein thrombosis (DVT)?

options.text: A clot in a superficial vein near the skin
A blood clot forming in a deep vein, usually in the leg, that can dislodge and cause a pulmonary embolism
A normal blood pooling in leg veins
An arterial blockage in the legs

quiz_questions.explanation: DVT is the formation of a thrombus in a deep vein, most commonly in the lower extremities, and its greatest danger is that the clot can break free and travel to the lungs, causing a potentially fatal pulmonary embolism.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the tunica intima?

options.text: The outer connective tissue layer of a blood vessel
The innermost layer of a blood vessel, consisting of a smooth endothelium
The middle smooth muscle layer of a blood vessel
The layer of valves found only in veins

quiz_questions.explanation: The tunica intima (or tunica interna) is the innermost layer of all blood vessels, composed of a thin endothelium that provides a smooth, friction-reducing surface for blood flow and plays a role in regulating clotting and inflammation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the tunica media?

options.text: The innermost endothelial layer of a blood vessel
The middle layer of a blood vessel, containing smooth muscle and elastic tissue
The outermost connective tissue layer of a blood vessel
A valve-containing layer found only in arteries

quiz_questions.explanation: The tunica media is the middle layer of blood vessels, composed primarily of smooth muscle cells and elastic fibers that allow vasoconstriction and vasodilation to regulate blood flow and pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the tunica adventitia (externa)?

options.text: The innermost endothelial layer
The middle smooth muscle layer
The outermost connective tissue layer that anchors the vessel to surrounding structures
A specialized valve layer within veins

quiz_questions.explanation: The tunica adventitia (or tunica externa) is the outermost layer of blood vessels, composed of collagen and elastic fibers that protect the vessel and anchor it to neighboring tissues and organs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'varicose veins' mean?

options.text: Veins that have become blocked by a blood clot
Veins that have become swollen, twisted, and enlarged due to incompetent valves allowing blood to pool
Arteries that have lost their elastic recoil
Capillaries that have burst under high pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: Varicose veins develop when venous valves become incompetent and fail to prevent backflow, causing blood to pool in the veins, which then become distended, tortuous, and visibly swollen, most commonly in the legs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'pulmonary circulation' mean?

options.text: Blood flow from the left ventricle to the body and back to the right atrium
Blood flow from the right ventricle to the lungs and back to the left atrium for gas exchange
Blood flow through the coronary arteries
Blood flow through the hepatic portal system

quiz_questions.explanation: Pulmonary circulation is the circuit that carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle through the pulmonary arteries to the lungs, where CO2 is released and O2 is picked up, then returns oxygenated blood via pulmonary veins to the left atrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 65-year-old man with a history of prolonged immobility after hip surgery develops sudden chest pain and difficulty breathing. Imaging reveals a clot that traveled from his leg to his lungs. What is the most likely sequence of events?

options.text: An arterial clot formed in his leg and traveled to the brain
A DVT in a deep leg vein dislodged and became a pulmonary embolism
A varicose vein ruptured, releasing a clot to the lungs
An aortic aneurysm burst, sending debris to the lungs

quiz_questions.explanation: Prolonged immobility is a major risk factor for deep vein thrombosis; the clot formed in a deep leg vein, broke free as an embolus, traveled through the venous system to the right heart, and lodged in a pulmonary artery causing a pulmonary embolism.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nurse notices a patient who has been standing for a 12-hour shift has visible, bulging veins on her lower legs. The patient reports aching and heaviness. What is the underlying cause of this condition?

options.text: Atherosclerotic plaque in the leg arteries
Incompetent venous valves allowing blood to pool in superficial veins
Excessive arteriolar vasoconstriction
A blood clot in the femoral artery

quiz_questions.explanation: Prolonged standing increases venous pressure in the legs, and when venous valves become incompetent, they fail to prevent backflow, causing blood to pool and veins to become dilated, tortuous, and visibly enlarged as varicose veins.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with severe atherosclerosis in both femoral arteries reports cramping leg pain whenever he walks more than one block, which resolves with rest. What is this symptom called and why does it occur?

options.text: Deep vein thrombosis; a clot is blocking venous return
Intermittent claudication; narrowed arteries cannot supply enough blood to working leg muscles
Varicose veins; blood is pooling in the legs
Venous insufficiency; valves in leg veins have failed

quiz_questions.explanation: Intermittent claudication is a hallmark symptom of peripheral artery disease in which atherosclerotic narrowing of the leg arteries restricts blood flow, causing ischemic muscle pain during exertion that resolves with rest when oxygen demand decreases.

quiz_questions.question_text: During an emergency, a paramedic needs to assess circulation in an unconscious patient's leg. She palpates the dorsalis pedis artery on the top of the foot. Why is checking a peripheral pulse important?

options.text: It measures the patient's blood pressure directly
It confirms that arterial blood flow is reaching the distal extremity
It evaluates venous return from the foot
It checks for deep vein thrombosis

quiz_questions.explanation: Peripheral pulses indicate that arterial blood is successfully being delivered to the extremities; an absent or weak dorsalis pedis pulse may indicate arterial obstruction or poor cardiac output threatening the limb.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pathologist examining a cross-section of an artery from a patient with severe hypertension notices the middle layer is significantly thickened. Which vessel layer has hypertrophied and why?

options.text: The tunica intima, because plaque has deposited there
The tunica media, because the smooth muscle thickened in response to chronically high pressure
The tunica adventitia, because connective tissue increased
The endothelium, because it expanded to hold more blood

quiz_questions.explanation: Chronic hypertension forces the smooth muscle cells in the tunica media to work harder and undergo hypertrophy (thickening), similar to how any muscle thickens with increased workload, which can further stiffen the artery.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is studying capillary exchange and wonders why capillaries are so effective at gas exchange while arteries are not. What structural feature makes capillaries uniquely suited for exchange?

options.text: Capillaries have thick muscular walls that actively pump substances across
Capillaries have walls only one endothelial cell thick, minimizing diffusion distance
Capillaries have valves that regulate the flow of nutrients
Capillaries are lined with smooth muscle for active transport

quiz_questions.explanation: Capillary walls consist of a single layer of endothelial cells with no tunica media or adventitia, creating an extremely thin barrier that allows rapid diffusion of gases, nutrients, and waste products between blood and interstitial fluid.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50-year-old patient is told that an abdominal aortic aneurysm was found incidentally on a CT scan. The aneurysm measures 5.5 cm. The surgeon recommends repair. Why is this finding concerning even though the patient has no symptoms?

options.text: It will inevitably cause a blood clot in the legs
Large aneurysms carry a significant risk of rupture, causing life-threatening internal hemorrhage
It indicates the patient already had a heart attack
Aneurysms block blood flow to the kidneys immediately

quiz_questions.explanation: Aortic aneurysms are often asymptomatic until they rupture, and the risk of rupture increases dramatically with size; a 5.5 cm aneurysm exceeds the threshold for surgical repair because rupture of the aorta causes massive, often fatal hemorrhage.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physiology instructor asks students why blood pressure is highest in the aorta and lowest in the vena cavae. Which answer best explains this pressure gradient?

options.text: The vena cavae are closer to the heart, so pressure drops
Friction and resistance in the vast network of vessels progressively reduce pressure as blood flows from arteries through capillaries to veins
Veins actively lower the pressure using their valves
The aorta has thinner walls that allow higher pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: Blood pressure drops progressively as blood moves through the circulatory system because friction between blood and vessel walls dissipates energy; the greatest pressure drop occurs in the arterioles (resistance vessels), leaving venous blood at very low pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient recovering from abdominal surgery is encouraged to wear compression stockings and perform ankle pump exercises while in bed. What is the primary purpose of these interventions?

options.text: To strengthen the arterial walls in the legs
To promote venous return and prevent deep vein thrombosis
To increase blood pressure to prevent fainting
To reduce arterial plaque buildup

quiz_questions.explanation: Compression stockings and ankle exercises activate the skeletal muscle pump and compress superficial veins, promoting venous return from the legs and reducing blood stasis, which is the primary risk factor for developing DVT during prolonged immobility.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physician explains to a patient that the blood from their intestines passes through the liver before entering general circulation. The patient asks why. What is the physiological advantage of the hepatic portal system?

options.text: It allows the liver to add extra oxygen to the blood before systemic distribution
It allows the liver to process absorbed nutrients, detoxify harmful substances, and regulate blood glucose before blood reaches the rest of the body
It reduces blood pressure in the GI tract
It stores excess blood for emergencies

quiz_questions.explanation: The hepatic portal system routes nutrient-rich blood from the GI tract through the liver first, enabling the liver to metabolize nutrients, store glycogen, detoxify harmful substances like alcohol and drugs, and regulate blood glucose before these enter systemic circulation.
